text: The use of traditional atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP) is frequently hampered in the development of biomaterials and in certain applications in microelectronics due to the limitations imposed by the presence of transition metals. We here demonstrate that the use of light as activation trigger and an organic catalyst in the form of a phenothiazine derivative allows for all-organic, metal-free ATRP. Studies using 10-phenylphenothiazine (PTH) as the activator/catalyst showed that the vinyl monomers could be polymerized in a controlled fashion (linear increase in molecular weight with conversion, low dispersity) and that the on/off switching capability of the reaction is very good. Furthermore, the low mass spectrometry data of crude polymers confirmed high chain end fidelity as in traditional transition metal catalyst-triggered ATRP. Using the PTH activator/catalyst, monomers that do not polymerize under the action of other photoredox catalysts, due to either insufficient activation, or due to the reduction step, which can degrade the polymerization process, could be polymerized and block copolymers with high structural regularity could be prepared.
